turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Hey, man, I've been thinking about this a lot lately. You know how everyone says money can't buy happiness? I totally disagree. Like, straight up, money is the key to everything. If you're rich, you can buy experiences, security, even friends if you want. Poor people are miserable because they worry all the time. What do you think?

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Whoa, that's a strong take, Alex. I mean, yeah, money helps with basics like food and a roof, but happiness? Isn't it more about relationships or purpose? I've known rich people who are super depressed.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Nah, see, those rich depressed people? They're just bad at spending it. Like, they buy dumb stuff or hang with fake people. If you know how to use money right—travel, therapy, whatever—you're set. Poor folks can't even afford therapy. It's all about options, dude.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Hmm, options... okay, fair. But, uh, what about lottery winners? Some go broke and miserable. Or billionaires who off themselves. Like, that one tech guy... uh, not naming names.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Lottery winners are idiots, mostly. They don't know how to manage it. And those suicides? Probably mental health issues that money can't fix alone, but c'mon, it gives you the best shot. Imagine being poor AND depressed—no doctors, no escapes. Money buys freedom from that grind.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Freedom, yeah... I get that. Tangent though—remember my uncle? Made bank in real estate, but his marriage tanked 'cause he was always working. Now he's loaded but lonely. Kids don't talk to him. Is that happiness?

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Lonely? He can hire companions, join clubs, whatever. Workaholics choose that. With money, you buy time later—retire early, fix relationships with gifts or vacations. Poor people can't even visit family 'cause of bus fares. It's relative, man.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Gifts and vacations... superficial much? Wait, no, I guess not always. But, like, studies show happiness plateaus after a certain income, right? Like $75k or something. After that, more money doesn't add much.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Those studies are BS. They're averaging everyone. For the top 1%, it keeps going up 'cause they live in luxury. $75k in New York? You're still scraping. Real wealth lets you ignore inflation, crime, all that. Happiness scales with security.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Okay, security point is solid. Hesitating here... but what about meaning? Like, artists or volunteers who are broke but fulfilled. My friend paints murals for free and swears he's happier than his banker brother.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Fulfillment without money is a fantasy. Your painter friend? One accident, he's homeless. Banker's got backup. Money amplifies meaning—you fund your passion without starving. Broke artists die young or quit.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Harsh, but... yeah, backups matter. Tangent: ever see those tiny house people? Happy on peanuts. Minimalism beats money?

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Tiny houses are cheap escapes for middle class. Try that in a real slum—no. Money lets you choose minimalism without suffering. It's the enabler.

turns.speaker: B
turns.raw_text: Alright, you're wearing me down. Maybe money isn't everything, but damn, it's a lot. Still think relationships trump it though.

turns.speaker: A
turns.raw_text: Relationships? Buy quality time with 'em. Money wins. Glad you see it.

input.objective: Debating whether money can buy happiness
